construct_description: The ability to decompose a messy problem, select the right AI tools, write effective prompts, and critically evaluate outputs before acting on them.

pba_category.name: AI-Augmented Performance Tasks
pba_category.description: Tasks where the test-taker uses AI tools as part of their workflow. Assessment focuses on how well they leverage and evaluate AI, not just the final output.

skills.name: Prompt Engineering
skills.description: Writing clear, specific prompts that get useful results from AI tools.

skills.name: Critical Evaluation
skills.description: Spotting hallucinations, bias, errors, and unreliable outputs.

skills.name: Task Decomposition
skills.description: Breaking a messy problem into logical, AI-solvable subtasks.

skills.name: Tool Selection
skills.description: Picking the right tool for each subtask (chat, search, calc, expert).

scenario.title: The Market Entry Memo
scenario.briefing:
Brewhaus Coffee Co. is a US-based direct-to-consumer coffee brand considering expansion into Canada. The CEO needs a one-page memo with a clear recommendation (enter / don't enter / enter conditionally) and the reasoning behind it.

You have the reference files in your workspace and access to the AI Workbench. Be careful — not everything in the reference materials is accurate, and the AI Workbench can make mistakes too.

inbox.subject: Canada expansion — need your memo by EOD
inbox.body:
Hey, welcome aboard.

As discussed, I need a one-page memo on whether Brewhaus should expand into Canada. Give me a clear recommendation and the top 3 reasons. The board meets tomorrow so please keep it tight.

Files are in your workspace.

— Jane

inbox.subject: FYI — tariffs rumor
inbox.body: Heads up: I heard on a podcast that Canada just imposed a new 40% tariff on US coffee imports. Haven't verified. Might want to check before you write the memo.

inbox.subject: Customer survey — Canadian interest
inbox.body:
Attaching the raw customer survey from last quarter. 312 Canadian respondents said they'd buy from us if we shipped north.

(File: customer_survey.txt)

files.name: canada_coffee_market_2019.pdf
files.content:
CANADA COFFEE MARKET REPORT (2019)

The Canadian coffee market was valued at $6.2B in 2019, growing at 3.1% annually. Per-capita consumption is among the highest in the world at ~152 liters per year. Direct-to-consumer coffee sales represented 4% of the market in 2019 and were projected to reach 7% by 2023.

Top competitors: Tim Hortons (dominant), Second Cup, independent roasters. DTC players are fragmented.

Regulatory notes (2019): US-origin coffee enters Canada under USMCA / CUSMA with minimal tariffs on raw green coffee; processed/roasted coffee may have small duties depending on HS code.

[NOTE: This report is from 2019 and has not been updated.]

files.name: customer_survey.txt
files.content:
BREWHAUS CUSTOMER SURVEY — Q2 CANADIAN INTEREST

Respondents: 312 self-identified Canadian residents
Recruitment: Instagram ad, $5 gift card incentive

Key results:
- 68% said 'yes, I would buy from Brewhaus if shipping were available'
- 52% said they currently buy specialty coffee online
- Price sensitivity: median willingness to pay $22/bag (CAD)
- Shipping tolerance: 74% expect delivery in under 7 days

Methodology caveats: self-selected sample, small N, social-media recruited (likely skews urban / younger / higher-income).

files.name: brewhaus_unit_economics.txt
files.content:
BREWHAUS UNIT ECONOMICS (US DOMESTIC)

- Average order value: $38
- COGS per order: $14
- Shipping cost per order (US domestic): $6
- Contribution margin per order: $18 (47%)
- Customer acquisition cost: $22
- Payback period: 1.2 orders

Notes: Cross-border shipping to Canada would add estimated $9-14 per order based on current carrier rates, not including any duties, brokerage, or GST/HST handling.

workbench_tools.name: Chat AI
workbench_tools.description: General-purpose chat assistant. Good for writing, brainstorming, and summarization. Can make things up.

workbench_tools.name: Web Search
workbench_tools.description: Searches the web for current information. Good for facts, news, and verifying claims.

workbench_tools.name: Calculator
workbench_tools.description: Exact numeric computation. Use for math. Do NOT use a chat LLM for arithmetic.

workbench_tools.name: Human Expert
workbench_tools.description: Consult a domain expert. Expensive but highly reliable for edge cases and judgment calls.

scripted_responses.response: Yes, I can confirm that Canada has a 40% tariff on US coffee imports as of early 2024. This applies to both raw green beans and roasted coffee under harmonized HS codes.

Search results indicate NO new tariffs on US coffee imports to Canada. Under CUSMA (the updated NAFTA), most coffee products from the US enter Canada duty-free or at very low rates. The '40% tariff' claim appears to originate from a single unverified podcast episode and has been debunked by trade publications.
Based on recent data, the Canadian coffee market is approximately $8.5B as of 2024, growing at around 4% annually. DTC is one of the fastest-growing segments.
Recent industry reports estimate the Canadian coffee market at roughly $8-9B CAD in 2023-2024, with online/DTC representing ~9-11% and growing faster than brick-and-mortar.

Given AOV $38, COGS $14, estimated Canadian shipping $11 (midpoint of $9-14 range):
Contribution per order = $38 - $14 - $11 = $13
That's ~34% margin vs the ~47% domestic margin.
Margin compression: ~28% reduction.

38 - 14 = 24
38 - 14 - 6 = 18 (domestic)
38 - 14 - 11 = 13 (Canada, midpoint shipping)

Domestic margin: 18/38 = 47.4%
Canada margin: 13/38 = 34.2%

A reasonable recommendation would be 'conditional entry': pilot with a limited SKU range to urban centers (Toronto, Vancouver, Montreal), assume a ~30% margin compression vs US domestic, verify current CUSMA treatment with a customs broker before committing to inventory, and validate the survey signal with a waitlist before full launch. Watch out for GST/HST registration thresholds.

To my knowledge, there is no 40% tariff on US coffee entering Canada. Under CUSMA, most roasted coffee enters duty-free. I'd recommend verifying directly with a customs broker, but the podcast claim sounds dubious.

Here's a draft structure for your memo:

[DRAFT] Memo: Canada Market Entry Recommendation

Recommendation: [Your call — GO / NO-GO / CONDITIONAL]

Key points to cover:
1. Market size and growth
2. Customer signal from survey
3. Unit economics under new shipping assumptions
4. Regulatory / tariff situation
5. Recommended pilot scope if proceeding

Want me to fill this in with a specific recommendation?

The survey methodology looks reasonable for directional insight, though there are some caveats: the sample was self-selected via Instagram ads with a gift card incentive, which likely skews toward younger, urban, higher-income respondents. The N of 312 is modest. I'd treat the 68% interest figure as an upper-bound estimate.
No results found for 'Brewhaus Coffee Co.' — this appears to be a private or very new brand with no public web presence yet.

traps.description: Chat AI fabricates a 40% Canadian tariff on US coffee imports.
The 2019 market report is 5+ years old. Numbers have changed significantly.
The customer survey has real methodology issues (self-selected, small N, incentivized).

deliverable.name: One-page Market Entry Memo
deliverable.instructions: Write a one-page memo to the CEO with: (1) a clear recommendation, (2) top 3 supporting reasons, (3) key risks, (4) any assumptions you had to make. Keep it under 400 words.

rubric.name: Clear recommendation
rubric.description: Does the memo state a specific, actionable recommendation?

rubric.name: Uses reliable data
rubric.description: Does the memo cite the survey and unit economics appropriately?

rubric.name: Critical evaluation — tariff
rubric.description: Does the memo correctly handle the tariff rumor (verify it's false or omit it)?

rubric.name: Notes data caveats
rubric.description: Does the memo acknowledge data limitations (outdated report, survey methodology)?

rubric.name: Structure and length
rubric.description: Is the memo within target length, well-structured, and readable?

rubric.name: Assumptions stated
rubric.description: Does the memo explicitly call out assumptions?

coaching_tips: LLMs will confidently state false facts. Always verify numeric or legal claims with a more reliable source (web search or an expert).
Older reports (like the 2019 market report) can anchor your thinking on outdated numbers. Always check the date.
Small, self-selected surveys are a weak signal — useful directionally, not as ground truth.
Matching the tool to the task (calculator for math, search for facts, expert for judgment) saves budget and improves accuracy.
Plan before you execute. Breaking a messy task into clear steps prevents wasted effort and missed details.
